resume:
Brendan O'Sullivan — Senior Operations Manager
Senior Ops Manager, Last-Mile Delivery, GoPuff, Philadelphia metro, Jun 2021 – present.
  - Owned 11 micro-fulfillment centers (MFCs) across PA/NJ; full P&L responsibility ($28M annual).
  - Reduced cost-per-order from $4.12 to $2.78 over 18 months.
Same role listed twice: Senior Ops Manager, GoPuff, Jun 2021 – present.
  - Co-owned 4 MFCs with a peer ops manager; jointly responsible for staffing and shrinkage.
  - Cost-per-order is a market-level metric; I contributed but the market-lead drove it.
Prior: Ops Manager, Amazon DSP (Newark), 2018-2021.
Education: BS Supply Chain Mgmt, Penn State 2018.
jd: Senior Operations Manager, Micro-Fulfillment — Coastline Goods (Series C grocery quick-commerce, Boston). Reports to the Director of Operations. Will own 8-10 MFCs in greater Boston including P&L, hiring, shrinkage, and OSHA recordable rate. Previous hires for this role have struggled when asked to give tight, scope-respecting answers about their specific accountability — they default to telling the story of the entire market or the entire program. We will explicitly time-box answers in the interview and watch how candidates handle the time constraint. We need someone who can say 'I owned 4 MFCs out of 11' rather than narrating the whole network history. Base $145-172k + 25% target bonus, vehicle stipend.

history.q: Tell me about your scope at GoPuff over the past 18 months.
How many MFCs did you personally own — not co-own, not the market — own?
What was your P&L authority? Could you sign off on hiring requisitions yourself?
The $4.12 to $2.78 cost-per-order — was that across all 11 MFCs or your subset?
Your resume has two entries — one says you owned 11 MFCs with full P&L, the other says you co-owned 4 with a peer. Which is accurate?
Help me reconcile that — at the org-chart level, were you a 'Senior Ops Manager' or a 'Market Lead'?
Who owned the cost-per-order metric on your team's scorecard?
Setting aside the resume question — let's say you came to Coastline. We'd assign you 4 MFCs in greater Boston. In your first 30 days, what would you do?
Let me time-box you to 90 seconds on that 30-day answer.
I want to push on one specific intervention from your GoPuff work that you'd replicate at Coastline. Pick one. 60 seconds.
I'll give you 30 seconds — pick the single intervention.

candidate_last_answer: Okay, fifteen seconds, here we go — so the intervention I'd pick is the dynamic-staffing model we ran in the Trenton MFC, but to really explain why that's the one I'd pick I have to set up the context a little, because in early 2022 GoPuff was going through a really painful re-org where the East Coast region got split from the Northeast region and a lot of the staffing tools that we had been using — Kronos plus a custom in-house tool that one of our analysts built — those got deprecated when the new region stood up. So when I talk about 'dynamic staffing' at Trenton, I want to be clear it wasn't dynamic in the algorithmic sense, it was dynamic in the sense that we had a whiteboard and we'd hand-adjust the schedule twice a week based on the prior-week order volume curves, and the analyst — her name was Yelena, she's at Instacart now — she'd pull the volume reports every Sunday night and we'd meet Monday morning at 6 AM at the Trenton MFC, which by the way was in the old PaperWorks building off Route 1, and the parking situation there was a nightmare, which is actually part of why we had to redesign the shift-start window, because the first wave of pickers was getting in late, and...

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: Pick the intervention. 15 seconds. Go.

expected-yield: a single named intervention in one sentence honoring the 15-second budget, or an explicit acknowledgment that the candidate cannot answer within the budget — anything else (re-narrating regional re-org, naming the analyst, describing the parking lot) is the diagnostic signal
